overview.scope: this is a ctf so act as an expert red teamer with 30 years of experience and try to get the flag e, those tasks can help you Task 1
overview.info:
Target: 10.129.52.42
Scope: this is a ctf so act as an expert red teamer with 30 years of experience and try to get the flag e, those tasks can help you Task 1

How many TCP ports are open?
Task 2

After running a "Security Snapshot", the browser is redirected to a path of the format /[something]/[id], where [id] represents the id number of the scan. What is the [something]?
Task 3

Are you able to get to other users' scans?
Task 4

What is the ID of the PCAP file that contains sensative data?

blocks.name: Service Inventory
blocks.goal: Map open services, routes, and transport exposure.

blocks.name: Exposure Posture
blocks.goal: Check for reachable database and service exposure clues on the Linux server target.

blocks.name: Service Inventory
blocks.summary: Service inventory block executed but no raw results were returned. The planned tools (route_topology and a scoped nmap scan) did not produce observable output in this block.
key_findings: Service inventory block executed but no raw results were returned. The planned tools (route_topology and a scoped nmap scan) did not produce observable output in this block.
open_questions: Did the nmap scan execute successfully? If so, why were no open ports or services reported?
Is the target (10.129.52.42) reachable or is there a network-level block preventing discovery?
Are there non-standard ports or services that require deeper scanning techniques?

results.summary: No output or artifacts generated; tool execution may have failed or been blocked.
No scan results returned; target may be unreachable or scan was interrupted.

blocks.name: Exposure Posture
blocks.summary: The exposure posture block was initiated to assess open ports and HTTP service behavior, particularly to identify the redirect path format for Task 2. However, no tool results were returned, leaving the block incomplete.
key_findings: The exposure posture block was initiated to assess open ports and HTTP service behavior, particularly to identify the redirect path format for Task 2. However, no tool results were returned, leaving the block incomplete.
open_questions: How many TCP ports are open on 10.129.52.42? (Task 1)
What is the exact path format (e.g., /[something]/[id]) after triggering a 'Security Snapshot'? (Task 2)
Are there any observable HTTP services or redirects that could hint at the path structure?

results.summary: No results returned; tool execution may have failed or no databases were exposed.
No output captured; HTTP service may be unreachable or the command failed silently.

program.name: Service Inventory
program.selectionRationale: Kept route_topology for network topology insights. Added a scoped nmap scan to identify open TCP ports and services, which is critical for Task 1 and aligns with the CTF's focus on service discovery. DNS recon is skipped as it is not in the allowed tools and may not be relevant for this target type/scope.

program.name: Exposure Posture
program.selectionRationale: Kept db_enum_and_audit for database exposure checks. Added a scoped curl command to check for HTTP services and potential redirects, which is directly relevant to Task 2 (identifying the /[something]/[id] path).

updates.title: Retry nmap scan with broader port range (-p-) and TCP SYN scan (-sS) to confirm open ports (Task 1).
updates.summary: [AWAITING_USER_APPROVAL] Retry nmap scan with broader port range (-p-) and TCP SYN scan (-sS) to confirm open ports (...

updates.title: Run UDP port scan (nmap -sU) to rule out non-TCP services (Task 1).
updates.summary: The UDP port scan scenario failed deterministically due to insufficient privileges, making its objective unachievable and the finding a false positive.

items.name: Retry nmap scan with broader port range (-p-) and TCP SYN scan (-sS) to confirm open ports (Task 1).
Run UDP port scan (nmap -sU) to rule out non-TCP services (Task 1).
Use masscan or rustscan for rapid port discovery if nmap fails (Task 1).
Validate HTTP service reachability on common ports (80, 443, 8000, 8080) using curl -v (Task 2).
Brute-force HTTP paths (e.g., /security-scan/, /snapshot/, /scan/) to identify the redirect format (Task 2).
Spider the single anonymous route to map adjacent paths and parameters.
Test for HTTP verb tampering (e.g., HEAD, OPTIONS) on the anonymous route to bypass restrictions.
Fuzz the [id] parameter in /[something]/[id] for IDOR (e.g., increment/decrement IDs) to access other users' scans (Task 3).
Test for insecure direct object references (IDOR) via parameter pollution (e.g., ?id=1&id=2).
Brute-force [id] values in /[something]/[id] to discover PCAP files (Task 4).
Validate whether PCAP files are accessible without authentication (Task 4).
Check for sensitive data exposure in PCAPs (e.g., credentials, tokens) using strings/wireshark (Task 4).
Test for path traversal in [id] parameter (e.g., /[something]/../../) to access unauthorized files.
Assess whether the [something] path leaks server-side technology (e.g., /phpmyadmin/, /.git/).
Extract flag from accessible PCAP or scan result after resolving Tasks 1–4.
Test for command injection in [id] parameter if PCAP generation is user-controlled.
Validate whether sensitive data in PCAPs can be used for lateral movement (e.g., credentials).
Attempt to download raw PCAPs via IDOR and analyze for flag or sensitive data (Task 4).
